coredata.dc:title: ZnO/carbon quantum dots heterostructure with enhanced photocatalytic properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yan
Zhang, Bo-Ping
Zhao, Jin-Xian
Ge, Zhen-Hua
Zhao, Xiao-Kun
Zou, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Zinc oxide (ZnO)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) heterostructure has been prepared via a sol–gel approach combined with a spin-coating processing and the CQDs obtained by a simple method. The samples are characterized by using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infra-red spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy and photocurrent–voltage curves, and the photocatalytic property of this heterostructure has also been investigated by using Rhodamine B as a test substance. The results revealed that some kind of electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs is formed. This interaction contributes to both the improvement of charge separation and the reduction of charge recombination, and thereby enhances the photocatalytic property. The degree of photocatalytic activity enhancement strongly depends on the layers of CQDs coating on the surface of ZnO layer. The heterostructure with 4 layers of CQDs shows the highest photocatalytic activity, which is about 3 times as that of pristine ZnO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Heterostructure
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) are TEM image and AFM image of CQDs, (c) is their size distributions corresponding of (a), (d) Vis–UV absorption spectra of CQDs, inset in (d) are photos of CQDs aqueous solution under visible and UV irradiation (365nm), respectively.
(a) TEM image of ZnO layer, the inset is a HRTEM image of single ZnO particle. (b) TEM image of ZnO/CQDs layer, and the inset is a HRTEM image of CQDs.
XRD patterns of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples.
XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples, (a) Zn-2p region and (b) O-1s region.
(a) and (b) FT-IR spectra of ZnO/CQDs sample and CQDs particles, respectively.
PL spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples under excitation wavelength at 360nm.
Photocurrent–voltage curves of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples with 4 layers of CQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO/CQDs samples with various layers of CQDs.

(a) The absorption spectra of RhB after photocatalytic degradation by various samples for 2h, (b) CQDs layer-dependence of photodegradation performance of RhB film after exposure to UV irradiation (365nm) for 2h (inset: the comparison images of RhB film before and after 2h photodegradation for the ZnO/CQDs sample with 4 layers of CQDs.
The schematic illustration of the mechanism for the photocatalysis of the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure.

head.ce:title: ZnO/carbon quantum dots heterostructure with enhanced photocatalytic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc oxide (ZnO)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) heterostructure has been prepared via a sol–gel approach combined with a spin-coating processing and the CQDs obtained by a simple method. The samples are characterized by using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infra-red spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy and photocurrent–voltage curves, and the photocatalytic property of this heterostructure has also been investigated by using Rhodamine B as a test substance. The results revealed that some kind of electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs is formed. This interaction contributes to both the improvement of charge separation and the reduction of charge recombination, and thereby enhances the photocatalytic property. The degree of photocatalytic activity enhancement strongly depends on the layers of CQDs coating on the surface of ZnO layer. The heterostructure with 4 layers of CQDs shows the highest photocatalytic activity, which is about 3 times as that of pristine ZnO.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental problems primarily caused by three industrial wastes which have been discharged without handled properly, are harmful for human health and hinder industrial and agricultural production. Photocatalyst, as an environmental scavenger, offers great potential for environmental purification through photocatalytic process. Wide-band gap semiconductor such as TiO2 and ZnO has been recognized as the excellent material for photocatalytic process because their large band gap [1–4], high photosensitivity, nontoxic nature, photochemical stability [5,6] and low cost [7,8]. Although TiO2 is extensively investigated and widely employed, it is proved that ZnO may exhibit a better efficiency than TiO2 in photocatalytic degradation for some dyes [9–13]. However, ZnO suffers from the drawback of poor quantum efficiency of photocatalysis caused by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [14,15] which limits the industrial application in photocatalysis. Therefore, several methods have been reported to reduce the recombination of charge carriers by coupling the photocatalyst with electron scavenging agents, such as noble metals [16–18], semiconductors [19,20], and carbon nano-materials [21–29].

Especially, carbon nanomaterials have attracted much attention in photocatalysis field due to their excellent electron transfer ability, good stability and dispensability, low cost and cytotoxicity [30–38]. Now, many efforts have been done on the preparation of photocatalyst composite using C60, graphene and carbon nanofiber as electron scavenging agents [23,39,40], but rarely work involved CQDs [28]. However, Sun and his coworkers [32] reported that the carbon dots had the characteristic of photo-induced electron transfer properties, which could be used in light energy conversion and related applications. And Zhen-Hui Kang's group found that the CQDs had up-conversion of PL properties, which could be used as a powerful energy-transfer component in photocatalyst design for meeting the demands of efficient use of sunlight [28]. Compared to other zero dimension carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots or C60, CQDs have lower cost and better dissolubility in water, and more methods to be obtained. In addition, the conduction band of ZnO is −4.05eV and valence band is −7.25eV (vs. vacuum) [41], while the work-function of CQDs is −4.5eV [42]. When electron–hole pairs generated in ZnO under UV light, such energy levels are beneficial for photo-induced electrons transferring from ZnO conduction band to CQDs, which will efficiently separate the photogenerated electron–hole pairs and hinder the charge recombination [43]. Therefore, it is expected that utilizing the above excellent properties of CQDs to prepare ZnO/CQDs heterostructure will offer a new opportunity for the improvement of charge separation and the reduction of charge recombination, so the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency.

Nowadays, the methods available to obtain CQDs are, including laser ablation of graphite [36,44], carboxylation of carbon nanotubes [45–47], derived from candle soot [48], proton-beam irradiation of nanodiamonds [49,50], microwave synthesis [35], hydrothermal synthesis [51], ultrasonic synthesis [52], template approach [53] and so on. However, these synthesis methods were limited by the requirement of special equipment, extremely expensive raw materials and multistep tedious process. In this work, we report a cheap and simple attempt for preparing CQDs, and then using CQDs to fabricate ZnO/CQDs heterostructure. The photocatalytic properties of these heterostructures are investigated by measuring the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) film as a test substance.

Graphite powder (99.9%) were purchased from Beijing Jixing Sheng’an Co. Ltd., Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (>99%), CH3OCH2CH2OH (99.989%), Na2CO3, H2SO4, HNO3 and Rhodamine B were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Deionized water was used in all preparations.

Graphite powder (0.3g) as the raw materials were directly added into a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (60mL) and HNO3 (20mL). The solution was successively sonicated for 2h and stirred for 24h at 80°C. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and diluted with deionized water (800mL). The pH value of the solution was adjusted to 8 by Na2CO3. The resulting solution was gray and contained CQDs and other large particles. After filtering by 0.2μm filter membrane and dialyzing with a cellulose ester membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 6 days, CQDs dispersed solution was obtained.

The ZnO/CQDs heterostructure has been prepared via a sol–gel approach combined with a spin-coating processing. First, ZnO precursor solution with a concentration of 0.1mol/L was prepared by mixing raw materials with Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and CH3OCH2CH2OH, followed by stirring for 2h and aging at room temperature for 24h. Then, ZnO layer was prepared by dropping the precursor solution on a quartz substrate performed at a rate of 3000rpm for 20s. After that, the layer was preheated at 250°C for 5min to remove the solvent and a small amount of water and annealed at 500°C in air for 30min to crystallize. Finally, the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure was obtained by dropping the CQDs dispersed solution onto the surface of the ZnO layer and annealed at 250°C in air for 2min. This preparation process was repeated different times for meeting various characterization demand.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed on JEM-100CXG and Philips Tecnai F20 electron microscope. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken using a Veeco D3100 atomic force microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by DMAX-RB with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15406nm) filtered through an Ni foil. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an PHI-5300 ESCA electron spectrometer using Al monochromatic Kα radiation (hv =1486.6eV) as the X-ray source. The binding energies were referenced to the C 1s line at 285.0eV from adventitious carbon. The UV–vis absorption and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs in water were measured with a UNIC UV-2800 spectrophotometer and a fluorimeter (SPEX flourolog-3) with slit width of 0.5mm. Fourier transform infra-red (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker spectrometer (Equinox 55/S) using KBr pellets. The photocurrent–voltage curves were recorded by using a CHI660E electrochemical station both in the dark and under illumination.

The photocatalytic activities of ZnO/CQDs heterostructures were evaluated by the degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation using an 18W ultraviolet lamp (Beijing institute of electrical light sources, China) with a cutoff filter (λ =365nm) and the heterostructures were illuminated through RhB layer. The RhB layers were spin coated onto the surface of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure at the speeding rate of 3000rpm. All samples were kept in the same layer thickness and reacted at room temperature in order to prevent any thermal catalytic effect. Before degradation, the equilibria of adsorption/desorption of RhB on ZnO/CQDs heterostructures were obtained by placing the samples statically in the dark for 2h, respectively. At given time intervals, the samples were analyzed by recording variations of the absorption band (550nm) in the UV–vis spectra.

Fig. 1a shows the TEM image of the collected CQDs, it is found that they are monodispersed and also that average particle size of the CQDs is in the range of 2–5nm (Fig. 1c), which are very similar to the one-step ultrasonic synthesized CQDs (2–3nm) [52]. AFM image (Fig. 1b) demonstrates their topographic heights are in the range of 3–5nm, suggesting that most of the CQDs consist of ca. 5–10 graphene layers [30]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs in water shows an absorption band at ca. 304nm with a narrow full width half maximum (fwhm) of 35nm and a shoulder at 352nm (Fig. 1d). Under irradiation by a 365nm lamp (16W), the CQDs emitted intense green-yellow luminescence (Fig. 1d, inset), which is similar to the graphene quantum dots [30] and probably attributable to their small size and surface states [54].

Fig. 2a depicts a TEM image of the pure ZnO layer prepared by a sol–gel and spin coating technique, while the inset of Fig. 2a shows a HRTEM image of single ZnO particle. After the addition of CQDs, the surface of pure ZnO layer becomes rougher and full of spherical nanoparticles as shown in Fig. 2b, and HRTEM image of this spherical nanoparticles reveals its graphitic layer structure (the inset of Fig. 2b). The distinct contrast between the ZnO and ZnO/CQDs sample provides sound evidence for the formation of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure.

Fig. 3a shows the XRD patterns for ZnO and ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples. For the ZnO sample, the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZnO are corresponding well to typical Wurtzite ZnO phase (PDF No. 36-1451) [55], suggesting a hexagonal ZnO crystal was synthesized after the annealing process. While for the ZnO/CQDs sample, a new diffraction peak centered at 26.22° attributing to (002) peak of graphite evidently appears, indicating that the CQDs were successfully coated on ZnO layer. Similar to ZnO–graphene composites [26], the enlarged XRD patterns for main peak of ZnO between 35° and 38° in Fig. 2b show that the diffraction peak shift to a lower degree because of the addition of CQDs. This shift demonstrates that maybe some kind of interaction between the CQDs and ZnO is formed and results in the increase of lattice constants of ZnO (from a = b =3.2433Å, c =5.1785Å for ZnO to a = b =3.2781Å, c =5.2261Å for ZnO/CQDs heterostructure by calculated). This interaction, as confirmed by XPS, FT-IR and PL spectroscopy investigations will be favorable for carrier separation and transport process and thus contribute to enhance photocatalytic efficiency.

To further investigate the presence of CQDs in the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure and the interaction between ZnO and CQDs, we measured the XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples. In Fig. 4a, the high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p for ZnO sample shows two symmetric peaks, which one centered at 1022.5eV corresponds to Zn 2p3/2 and another one centered at 1045.5eV is assigned to Zn 2p1/2, indicating the Zn2+ species is in a completely oxidized state [56]. However, for the ZnO/CQDs sample, Zn 2p3/2 bond is divided into two parts and the binding energy of Zn 2p1/2 slightly decrease, which can be attributed to the change of bond structures between Zn and other atoms existed in the sample [57]. Here, the new shoulder peak maybe ascribe to the Zn(2−x)+ species. Chang Kim and his coworkers [39] reported that Ti3+ species was generated due to the chelation between TiO2 and the carboxyl groups of grapheme/carbon composite nanofibers, and the increasing of Ti3+ state on the TiO2 surface in favor for separation of carriers and improvement of photocatalysis efficiency. Similarly, the arise of Zn(2−x)+ species may be attributed to the carboxyl groups of CQDs. Some of these groups combine with Zn2+ through chelation, and the positions of the oxygen in ZnO are occupied by carboxyl groups. In Fig. 4b, the O-1s peak of the ZnO/CQDs sample is shifted from 530.8 to 531.6eV compared with ZnO samples, implying that some of the ZnO bonds have been transferred into ZnCOO bonds [57]. And also verifies the presence of electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs. It is notably that the Zn(2−x)+ species has not been reported previously based on ZnO hybridized other carbon nano-materials [26,58], implying that the interaction between the CQDs and ZnO particles is stronger in present work.

The presence of electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs can be further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, the strong peaks of ZnO sample at 500, 748 and 876cm−1 are attributed to ZnO stretching vibrations [59,60] and the absorption bands observed at around of 1600 and 3275cm−1 of ZnO/CQDs sample to the characteristic peaks of CO and OH stretching vibrations, the peak at 1390cm−1 to the symmetric carboxylate stretch [61,62]. Notably, it can be seen that the vibration peak at 876cm−1 in Fig. 5a shows a little blue shift and a new peak which can be attributed to carbonyl stretching vibration appeared at about 1730cm−1 after the addition of CQDs. Furthermore, compared with the FT-IR spectra of pure CQDs sample (Fig. 5b), the CO and OH stretching vibrations absorption peaks also shows a blue shift for ZnO/CQDs sample and the intensities of them clearly weaker. From these results, it can be deduced that a new ZnCOO− group is formed in the ZnO/CQDs sample, since the formation of a new group will manifest a new absorption peaks in FT-IR spectra and also affect adjacent groups’ electron distribution or cause nonlocal electron rearrangement, resulting in FT-IR blue shift. Moreover, the degree of blue shift is more obvious for the CQDs because of their small size and almost functional groups located in the edge.

In order to further verify the electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs and investigate the transfer behavior of the photogenerated electrons and holes in the composite, the PL emission spectra (excited at 360nm) and the photocurrent–voltage curves for ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples have been studied. The measured PL emission spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples in the range of 380–600nm are presented in Fig. 6 . The spectra of ZnO sample exhibit a strong emission at 400nm in the near-ultraviolet (NV) region and two small peaks at 425 and 470nm in visible regions. The NV peak is due to the free recombination of exciton centers [63–65]. The peak at about 425nm maybe originating from a different deep-level emission located in the band gap such as oxygen vacancies and/or zinc interstitials [66,67]. The blue-green light emission at 470nm is possible due to surface defects in the ZnO [63,68]. Notably, the PL intensity of ZnO decreases obviously after the addition of CQDs, indicating that the recombination of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs is inhibited greatly in the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure, which can be attributed to effective charge transfer between ZnO and CQDs. Fig. 7 shows the photocurrent–voltage curves for ZnO and ZnO/CQDs samples. In the dark, the current are almost the same for both samples. However, under light illumination (365nm), the current of ZnO/CQDs sample is overall higher than that of the ZnO sample through the bias potential range from −1.0 to 1.0V, indicating a higher charge separation efficiency of photo-induced holes and electrons. The results of PL spectra and photocurrent–voltage curves demonstrate a direct electron/hole transfer between the ZnO and CQDs through photo-induced charge transfer transitions, which could be attributed to the electronic interaction of CQDs and ZnO semiconductor [28].

Fig. 8 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples with different CQDs contents, as well as the pure ZnO sample. Because ZnO and CQDs in the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure are two different phases, their band-gap energies are not changed [58]. Thus both the absorption of ZnO and CQDs can be detected in the UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples. Whereas, except for absorption band at 360nm of ZnO, no absorption peaks are observed for CQDs absorption in the ZnO/CQDs sample, which may ascribed to their low content and weak absorption intensity in the heterostructure. Moreover, the intensity of the absorption peaks of ZnO almost keep the same with increasing the layers of CQDs, indicating the introduction of CQDs does not influence optical adsorption property of ZnO.

To demonstrate the potential application of the as-obtained ZnO/CQDs heterostructure for the degradation of organic pollutants, we carried out the experiments of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB as a model reaction. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activities of pure ZnO sample and ZnO/CQDs heterostructure samples with various CQDs content are also provided for comparison and searching the best content of CQDs in the heterostructure, respectively. The absorption spectra of RhB after photocatalytic degradation by various samples are shown in Fig. 9a, and the influence of layers of CQDs on the photodegradation rate of RhB is shown in Fig. 9b. The blank test confirms that RhB is not degraded irradiated under UV light at the absence of the catalyst, while 30% of RhB can be photodegraded by ZnO after 2h irradiation. Obviously, all the ZnO/CQDs heterostructures exhibit much higher photocatalytic activities than that of ZnO. Especially, the sample with 4 layers of CQDs shows the highest activity, 80% of RhB can be photo-degraded under the same conditions, which enhanced by about 3 times with respect to that of the ZnO sample. So the best layer of CQDs for this heterostructure is 4.

The higher photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure than ZnO simple indicates that the electronic interaction by chelation between ZnO and CQDs is important, which can significantly increase the separation of photogenerated charge and enhance the transfer of electron–hole pairs. The improvement of photocatalytic activity of ZnO after CQDs coated can be attributed to the efficient charge separation caused by the rapid photo-induced charge transfer [58] and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs as confirmed by PL spectra and photocurrent–voltage curves. The efficient charge separation can increase the lifetime of the charge carriers and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs will enhance the efficiency of the interfacial charge transfer to adsorbed substrates, and thus increase the number of carriers adsorbed by contaminant. In the present work, the mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of RB is proposed as follows:
ZnO / CQDs + h v →
e −
( CQDs ) +
h +
( ZnO )
e −
+
O 2
→
O 2
⋅ −
h +
+
OH −
→ OH ⋅
O 2
⋅ −
+
H 2
O →
HO 2
⋅ +
OH −
HO 2
⋅ +
H 2
O →
H 2
O 2
+ OH ⋅
H 2
O 2
→ 2 OH ⋅
OH ⋅ + RhB →
CO 2
+
H 2
O
When the ZnO/CQDs heterostructure is irradiated by UV light with the energy higher than or equal to the band-gap of ZnO, the electrons (e−) in the valence band of ZnO can be excited into the conduction band and followed by move freely to the surface of CQDs layer through photo-induced charge transfer process, meanwhile, the holes (h +) leave in the valence band of ZnO. Subsequent to various steps, the holes (h +) migrate to the surface and react with H2O or OH to produce OH· radicals. The photo-generated electrons can react with the dissolved oxygen on the surface of CQDs to form radicals O2·−, which on protonation generate the hydroperoxy HO2· radicals, producing hydroxyl radical OH·, which was a strong oxidizing agent to decompose organic dye [62,69,70]. The schematic illustration of the mechanism for the photocatalysis of the ZQDs-CQDs heterostructure is summarized in Fig. 10 .

In summary, we afford a simple method to obtain CQDs and prepare ZnO/CQDs heterostructure via a sol–gel approach combined with a spin-coating process. The heterostructure of ZnO/CQDs can significantly increase the photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO. The degree of photocatalytic activity enhancement strongly depended on the layers of CQDs nanoparticles, and the sample of ZnO/CQDs heterostructure with 4 layers of CQDs shows the highest photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic activity for ZnO/CQDs heterostructure is attributed to the efficient charge separation and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs due to the electronic interaction between ZnO and CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51202011) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRT-TP-13-009A).
